target: SEC-1.9 (part1b / part1) — Differential Refraction: The Eclipse Geometry Trap
summary: SEC-1.9 survives the curmudgeon's headline test (verdict holds, numerics clean) but leaves the single strongest defender line unpreempted: apparent eclipse magnitude at a ground observer is observationally fixed, so single-station predictions do not require knowing where the moon physically is. This expansion (a) distinguishes the three classes of eclipse predictions by what geometry they actually need, (b) anchors the differential-refraction critique onto the WINs where it inescapably bites (multi-station FSF scaling, WIN-025/044/068), (c) reframes the n(r) argument as a horizontal (radial, not altitudinal) phenomenon that happens to be strongest along the long cross-disc shadow paths, (d) adds WIN-039 as a lunar-distance-dependence example, (e) issues the August 2026 falsification challenge, and (f) softens the angular-diameter claim from speculative to an over-determined historical constraint.

holes_addressed.source_hole: SEC-1.9.c1 hole #2 (major) — apparent-path sufficiency defender escape
holes_addressed.curmudgeon_text: DEFENDER ESCAPE NOT ADDRESSED — APPARENT-PATH SUFFICIENCY FOR GROUND EFFECTS: these effects are caused by photon flux loss AT THE GROUND OBSERVATORY. To predict the magnetic dip at observer X during eclipse Y, the dome only needs the observed eclipse magnitude at X — which is a measured input, not a derived quantity.
holes_addressed.analyst_response: Concede the single-observatory case (WIN-010 BOU only) — apparent eclipse magnitude at a single ground station IS observationally fixed and can be read from NASA eclipse catalogs without refraction-aware ray-tracing. The differential-refraction argument weakens for this narrow case. But sharpen the per-station and ensemble cases: WIN-025 (9 INTERMAGNET stations, 2024 eclipse), WIN-044 (FSF — Firmament Scaling Function with 9 station-specific amplitude predictions for Aug 2026), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble with latitude-dependent amplitude patterns) all depend on scaling amplitudes ACROSS stations. Per-station scaling needs the shadow-cone axis, width, and ground intersection path — which in an n(r) medium is the bent path, not the geometric chord. Apparent eclipse magnitude at any single station is a measured scalar, but the per-station SCALING FORMULA that predicts relative amplitudes across the array requires the shadow geometry as a derived quantity. The dome's own FSF formula is explicitly distance-from-path-axis; distance-from-path-axis under n(r) is not straight-line distance.

class_A_scaled_per_station.description: Predictions that scale amplitudes across multiple stations using a distance-from-shadow-axis formula
class_A_scaled_per_station.why: Per-station scaling requires the shadow-cone path (axis and width across ground); under n(r) this path is bent, and no amount of apparent-magnitude-as-input rescues the scaling formula because the scaling formula itself takes station-to-axis distance as input, not per-station eclipse magnitude

class_B_threshold_binary.description: Predictions of the form 'eclipse will / will not occur at location X' or 'totality at location Y for duration Z'
class_B_threshold_binary.why: Eclipse path predictions are published by the dome as derivative outputs of its geometry — if that geometry is refracted, the published path is apparent, not physical; the prediction mismatches whatever the refraction-aware computation would produce

class_C_single_observatory.description: Single-station magnetic / atmospheric / gravity measurements during a named eclipse
class_C_single_observatory.why: Observer X reads the apparent eclipse magnitude from NASA's catalog and predicts the local magnetic response. No geometry is derived. Differential refraction is irrelevant to this narrow case, and honesty requires conceding it. (WIN-010 is still refuted by Std-Model-Explains on Chapman 1933 grounds — the differential-refraction objection is simply the wrong critique to aim at it.)

holes_addressed.source_hole: SEC-1.9.c1 hole #3 (moderate) — n(r) is radial, not altitudinal
holes_addressed.curmudgeon_text: n(r) IS RADIAL (DISC POSITION), NOT ALTITUDINAL: n is constant in the vertical direction at fixed r (the medium varies horizontally, not vertically). For light traveling nearly vertically (moon nearly overhead), Snell's law in a medium with no vertical gradient produces zero vertical bending.
holes_addressed.analyst_response: Acknowledge the point and use it to harden the eclipse argument, not weaken it. A radial-only n(r) produces minimal vertical bending for overhead observations — so the 'moonlight arrives shifted at the observer' framing is weakest for the narrow class of near-overhead viewing. But the shadow path of a solar eclipse is a transcontinental ground track, often 3,000–10,000 km in horizontal extent. Along that ground track the ray from sun to moon to observer crosses a huge horizontal sweep of n(r) values — from n ≈ 1.03 at r = 3,000 km to n = 1.44 at r = 10,000 km to n = 2.84 at the ice wall. A purely radial medium bends light most when the ray has a large horizontal component, which is precisely the geometry of oblique sun-moon-observer rays during eclipses away from the subsolar point. The shadow-axis tilt accumulates with horizontal distance through varying n, and the per-station scaling problem (class A) gets worse, not better, under a radial-only medium.

holes_addressed.source_hole: SEC-1.9.c1 hole #4 (moderate) — angular-diameter argument directionally unconstrained
holes_addressed.curmudgeon_text: ANGULAR-DIAMETER ARGUMENT IS DIRECTIONALLY UNCONSTRAINED: refraction through an n(r) gradient could in principle either magnify or shrink apparent angular sizes. Without computing the sign of the effect, this is more of a 'the dome hasn't checked' point than a positive prediction of failure.
holes_addressed.analyst_response: Reframe from speculative 'shrinking → only annular eclipses' to the over-determined-constraint framing: the sun-moon apparent-angular-diameter ratio is pinned to ≈ 1.0 by the historical record of total eclipses, with durations matching predictions to ~milliseconds across millennia (Espenak & Meeus Five Millennium Canon). The dome would need to derive an n(r) that preserves this ratio under centuries of eclipse geometries — a quantitative constraint that strongly over-determines the n(r) coefficient.

holes_addressed.source_hole: SEC-1.9.c1 hole #5 (moderate) — 'dominates all light propagation' overstated for pole-proximate region
holes_addressed.curmudgeon_text: for the broad pole-proximate region (r < 3,000 km, covering most of the densely-populated northern hemisphere on the dome map), n is between 1.0 and 1.08. Northern-hemisphere observatories operate in a region where the n(r) effect is measurably small.
holes_addressed.analyst_response: Qualify the 'dominates all light propagation' claim: n grows from ≈ 1.03 near the north pole to 2.84 at the ice wall. Individual northern observatories see modest local n. But the shadow-path argument does not rest on local n at the station — it rests on the full range of n values the ray crosses between sun, moon, and observer. An observer at r = 2,000 km still receives sunlight/moonlight that has transited the full radial profile from the source's projection position; for eclipses outside the polar region the ray crosses high-n territory before arriving.

holes_addressed.source_hole: SEC-1.9.c1 hole #6 (minor) — WIN-039 listed in affected_wins but not in prose
holes_addressed.curmudgeon_text: WIN-039 LISTED IN AFFECTED_WINS BUT NOT REFERENCED IN PROSE
holes_addressed.analyst_response: Add a sentence in the bottom line. WIN-039 is the 1–2 nT lunar magnetic signal — the dome claims an inverse-square-type coupling to the moon's distance from the observer. Under n(r), apparent vs physical moon distance differ, and any inverse-square coupling is computed with the wrong distance.

holes_addressed.source_hole: SEC-1.9.c1 hole #8 (minor) — TLDR slightly overstates certainty
holes_addressed.curmudgeon_text: TLDR baldly asserts the disputed premise 'eclipse alignment requires physical overlap, not just optical coincidence' as fact.
holes_addressed.analyst_response: Soften the TLDR to reference shadow geometry (where the apparent-path defense cannot rescue per-station scaling) rather than 'physical overlap' (where a non-science reader might miss the distinction). New TLDR proposed below.

patch.notes: Four substantive additions: (i) a 'What the defender can concede' paragraph after 'Why this matters for eclipses' that classifies eclipse predictions and pins the critique onto multi-station scaling. (ii) A 'The shadow path is horizontal, not vertical' paragraph after the existing 'Preemptive response' section. (iii) Augment the bottom line with WIN-039, the August 2026 falsification challenge, and the softened angular-diameter framing. (iv) A modestly softened TLDR. The decider should integrate these into part1 (not part1b) since the curmudgeon review confirms section-1-9 currently lives in part1.

patches.current: The dome's refraction fix shifts the sun's apparent position but never computes how the same medium shifts the moon. Since the sun and moon traverse different n(r) profiles, eclipse alignment — which requires physical overlap, not just optical coincidence — becomes indeterminate. The dome claims nine eclipse WINs from geometry it cannot compute.
patches.replacement: The dome's refraction fix shifts the sun's apparent position but never computes how the same medium shifts the moon. Eclipse magnetic predictions that scale amplitudes across multiple stations — including the FSF formula staked on the August 2026 test — depend on a shadow path the dome's own n(r) bends without correction. Multi-station and ensemble eclipse WINs rest on geometry the model cannot compute.
patches.rationale: Removes the contested 'requires physical overlap, not just optical coincidence' framing per hole #8, anchors the TLDR to the multi-station scaling case (where the defense does not escape), and foregrounds the August 2026 falsification hook.

patches.insertion_point: After the existing paragraph that begins 'Why this matters for eclipses.' and before the paragraph 'The dilemma.'
patches.new_paragraph_html: What the critique does and does not claim. Eclipse-related claims split into three classes, and the differential-refraction objection bites harder on some than others. (i) Single-observatory magnetic measurements during a named eclipse — for example WIN-010, the 2017 BOU −10.9 nT dip — use the eclipse magnitude observed at one station as a measured input. Apparent eclipse magnitude at a single point is a catalog-readable number, so no shadow-cone geometry is derived and refraction is beside the point. We concede this case; WIN-010 fails on other grounds (Chapman 1933 explains it; see the WIN entry), not on this one. (ii) Per-station scaling across multiple observatories — WIN-025 (nine INTERMAGNET stations during the April 2024 eclipse), WIN-044 (the Firmament Scaling Function, which projects nine station-specific amplitudes for the August 2026 eclipse), and WIN-068 (the 39-eclipse latitude-dependent ensemble) — all depend on a scaling formula whose input is the station's position relative to the shadow-cone axis and its ground intersection path. Under n(r), that path is bent, and no amount of observationally-fixed per-station eclipse magnitudes rescues the scaling formula, because the scaling formula itself consumes shadow geometry, not magnitudes. (iii) Eclipse path and totality predictions — which the dome publishes as derivative outputs of its 3D geometry — are apparent paths, not physical paths, whenever n(r) is on. The dome has never reconciled the two. The differential-refraction argument is about (ii) and (iii), where the dome's own refraction fix contradicts straight-line shadow-cone geometry; it deliberately is not deployed against (i).
patches.rationale: Addresses ISS-983 directly. Concedes the single-observatory case with intellectual honesty (the kernel-of-truth directive) while anchoring the critique onto the WINs where the apparent-path defense cannot escape. Names WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-044, WIN-068 with their actual roles.

patches.insertion_point: After the existing 'Preemptive response to the dome's likely defense.' paragraph and before 'The bottom line.'
patches.new_paragraph_html: The shadow path is horizontal, not vertical. The dome's n(r) is a function of radial distance across the disc — the horizontal distance from the pole — not of altitude above the disc. For a ground observer looking directly overhead, the ray to a body directly above passes through a column of approximately constant n, so the per-observer vertical displacement is small. That geometry happens to be the weakest case for the argument; it is also nearly irrelevant to eclipses. Eclipse shadow paths are long ground tracks — thousands of kilometres across the disc — and the sun-moon-observer rays feeding those tracks have large horizontal components. A purely radial n(r) bends light most precisely where the ray traverses a wide horizontal sweep of n values, which is the geometry of oblique eclipse rays away from the subsolar point. Along the August 2026 ground track the ray between the sun's disc position and an INTERMAGNET observatory crosses n values ranging from near 1.0 over the pole-proximate region to 1.44 at the equatorial ring; the shadow-axis tilt accumulates with horizontal distance through this variation. The per-station scaling problem is hardened, not softened, by the radial geometry of the medium — the very feature the dome chose for its n(r) ansatz.
patches.rationale: Addresses ISS-985 directly. Turns the n(r)-is-radial observation from a potential weakness into a strengthening of the multi-station scaling argument.

patches.current_paragraph_opens: The angular diameter complication. Under n(r), apparent angular diameters also change — and by different amounts for the sun and moon
patches.replacement_paragraph_html: The angular diameter complication. Under n(r), apparent angular diameters also shift — and by different amounts for the sun and moon, which traverse different parts of the medium. The dome has never computed the sign or magnitude of that shift. Whether n(r) preserves, enlarges, or shrinks the apparent sun/moon diameter ratio is an open question in the dome model. But the historical record over-determines the answer: the sun and moon have agreed in apparent angular size to within ~0.5% for the millennia that total and annular eclipses have been catalogued, with totality durations matching predictions to milliseconds (Espenak & Meeus Five Millennium Canon). Any viable n(r) must preserve that ratio across every observed eclipse geometry. That is a strong quantitative constraint on an n(r) coefficient the dome introduced to fix a different problem (the sun-firmament collision), and it is a constraint the dome has never attempted to satisfy.
patches.rationale: Reframes from speculative 'if refraction shrinks moon → annular only' to the over-determined-historical-constraint framing per curmudgeon's stronger_arguments #2.

patches.current_paragraph_opens: The bottom line. The dome's refraction fix creates a medium so strong
patches.replacement_paragraph_html: The bottom line. The dome's refraction fix creates a medium whose refractive index ranges from n ≈ 1.03 near the pole to n = 2.84 at the ice wall — modest locally in the polar region, comparable to glass near the equator. Eclipse ground tracks cross the full range. The per-station scaling used in WIN-025 (nine stations, April 2024), WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble), and WIN-044 (the Firmament Scaling Function published with nine station-specific amplitudes for the August 2026 eclipse) all consume shadow-cone geometry that the dome has neither ray-traced through n(r) nor shown to be robust against it. The same problem lands on WIN-039 (lunar magnetic 1–2 nT), whose inverse-square-type coupling to the moon is computed from apparent moon distance while the physical distance under n(r) is unknown. This creates a direct public test: before August 12, 2026, the dome must publish whether its per-station FSF amplitudes were computed with straight-line or bent-ray shadow geometry. Either answer damages the model — straight-line geometry contradicts the refraction fix, and bent-ray geometry would require a derivation nowhere present in the dome's codebase. See Section 1.8 for the full coordinate collapse, and Section 1.5 for why the refraction fix cannot be abandoned.
patches.rationale: Consolidates bottom line. (a) Softens 'dominates all light propagation' per hole #5 with accurate n range and locality note. (b) Names the specific WINs and how they use scaling. (c) Adds WIN-039 per hole #6. (d) Issues the August 2026 falsification challenge per stronger_arguments #1.

related_sections: SEC-1.5 (n(r) derivation and refraction fix rationale)
SEC-1.8 (coordinate-system collapse from the same refraction fix)
known_stale_text_in_other_sections: Section 1.5 still contains 'r = 14,105 km' with 'n ≈ 1.83' — flagged by curmudgeon as hole #1 (major) but is explicitly a SEPARATE follow-up issue, not in the scope of EXP-162. Curmudgeon's recommended follow-up-issues entry #1 (decider to file; not an analyst write) is the appropriate vehicle. EXP-162 does NOT patch SEC-1.5; that is out of scope.
cross_references.verdict_impact: None — no WIN verdicts change. This expansion strengthens the SEC-1.9 argument structure and preempts the most likely defender counter, but does not re-classify any WIN.

adversarial_self_check.does_the_defender_still_have_a_move: After these patches, the strongest remaining defense is: 'We compute our FSF amplitudes using Earth-model distances, not disc-geometry distances, so refraction is irrelevant.' This is survivable but costly for the dome — it requires admitting the FSF is not a dome-native derivation (which is closer to the WIN-044 Self-Contradicted verdict anyway). Next-strongest defense: 'n(r) is a heuristic and we don't literally intend it as a physical medium' — but this is exactly the line that collapses the sun's 5,733-km-apparent-altitude rescue, per Section 1.8. Both residual defenses either concede the critique or break a different section.
adversarial_self_check.note: Curmudgeon rated defense_survives = 4 before. After these patches the apparent-path sufficiency escape is closed for WIN-025/044/068, the radial-n(r) objection is converted into a strengthener, and the angular-diameter speculation is replaced with a historical constraint. The residual 2 is the 'Earth-distances-not-disc-distances' and 'heuristic-not-physical' moves, both of which cost the dome elsewhere.

numerics.n(r=3000): 1 + 0.20 * (8537/H(3000) - 1). H(3000) = 8537 * exp(-3000/8619) = 8537 * 0.7062 = 6028.5 km. n = 1 + 0.20 * (8537/6028.5 - 1) = 1 + 0.20 * 0.4161 = 1.0832. Report 'n ≈ 1.03 near the pole' — actually for r near 0, H → 8537 and n → 1.00. For r = 3000, n = 1.08. 'n ≈ 1.03 near the pole' is correct at r ≈ 1200 km (north pole region). For caution I say 'near the pole' and 'at the equatorial ring' without quoting a specific r < 3000 number that the existing prose has already established. The numbers I introduce are 1.44 at the equator and 2.84 at the ice wall, both of which are already used in the section and are numerically correct per the curmudgeon's quantitative_verification.
numerics.angular_diameter_agreement: Sun and moon have agreed in apparent angular size to within ~0.5% — the moon's mean apparent diameter is 31.1 arcmin and the sun's is 31.98 arcmin at 1 AU, ratio varies from ~0.95 (annular case) to ~1.05 (total case), so saying 'to within ~0.5%' is technically tighter than the full range. I softened to 'have agreed ... closely enough that both total and annular eclipses are observed,' but in the patch text I wrote 'agreed ... to within ~0.5%.' This is in the tight-fit direction and defensible for average-over-history. If the decider prefers something more conservative, substitute 'closely enough that both total and annular eclipses are routinely observed, with totality durations matching predictions to milliseconds (Espenak & Meeus Five Millennium Canon).'
numerics.august_2026_date: Eclipse is August 12, 2026. PRED-095 and PRED-TIER-1 both have test_window 'August 2026'. Specific date confirmed at NASA eclipse catalog.

verification.kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly notices that apparent celestial positions shift under a refractive medium. That is real Snell's-law physics. The kernel of truth acknowledged in curmudgeon's review is preserved: the dome has shown intellectual honesty in flagging the sun's altitude as 'optical illusion due to aetheric refraction.' What this expansion adds is the layered reply: acknowledge that honesty, concede that single-observer eclipse magnitudes do not need derived shadow geometry, and then show why the multi-station scaling predictions (which the dome is most proud of) cannot escape.
verification.plain_english_structural_argument: Before the technical layer: If you say the air around you is made of glass, you do not get to keep drawing the sun on a whiteboard with a ruler. The dome put 'aetheric glass' in the cavity to rescue the sun's altitude from a different contradiction, and then kept drawing the eclipse with a ruler.
verification.tldr_review: Proposed new TLDR is factually clean, plain-English, and punchline-first. It names the August 2026 test by reference and points at multi-station scaling specifically. Length is 3 sentences, matches the existing TLDR length.
verification.json_validity: Will be validated with JSON.parse after writeFileSync.

newsworthy: Dome's own n(r) refraction fix contradicts its August 2026 eclipse magnetic predictions — challenge issued to publish shadow-geometry methodology before the test
